narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put considerable effort into your application, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 28. Your Year 1 school decile of 6 is right around the typical student's performance, showing you held your own early on. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 65% chance that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. The biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile of 1, which stands out well below the average of around the middle of the pack and signaled a significant drop in performance compared to most students. This outweighed other elements, like your undergraduate GPA, which was slightly below average but helped nudge the prediction a bit toward passing by showing decent overall academic ability. Your LSAT score, lower than the typical 36.7, also contributed toward the failure prediction, though less strongly than your later-year grades. Meanwhile, your stronger Year 1 decile, family income bracket, and full-time enrollment status each provided some positive counterbalance, aligning with or better than what the model sees in successful bar passers. In essence, while you started strong and have supportive circumstances, the sharp decline in your Year 3 ranking was the dominant concern, setting you apart from applicants whose grades trended upward or stayed consistently high. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how your profile stacked up against patterns in past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your growth and resilience. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas, and with your full-time commitment, you're well-positioned to turn this around. Keep pushing—you've got this.
